planting.note: Direct sow seeds or transplant container-grown plants in well-drained, lean to moderately fertile, slightly alkaline to neutral soil in full sun; lion's tail is fast-growing from seed and may flower in its first season. It is a large, woody-based sub-shrub reaching 4–6 feet in mild climates; in zones 8 and colder it dies back in winter but regrows strongly from the base. Outstanding for hummingbirds and butterflies. The leaves and flowers have traditional medicinal uses in South Africa, where 'dagga' is used colloquially (not to be confused with Cannabis).

directions: Plant in well-drained, lean to moderately fertile, slightly alkaline to neutral soil in full sun; lion's tail is one of the most visually striking tall sub-shrubs for warm climate gardens — a South African native producing from late summer through autumn and into winter (September through February in mild climates) long, upright, square-stemmed shoots bearing at regular intervals dense, globular whorls of velvety-textured, tubular, two-lipped, brilliant deep-orange to orange-scarlet flowers — the stacked orange rings of flowers arranged up the stem creating the 'lion's tail' silhouette that gives the plant its common name. Each individual flower is a vivid, velvet-textured, deep orange tube about 2 inches long projecting upward from the whorl, and a large plant in full bloom with multiple stems bearing stacked rings of vivid orange is one of the most spectacular displays in the subtropical garden. The color is a specific, saturated, warm orange-to-vermillion that attracts hummingbirds powerfully — migrating hummingbirds on the Gulf Coast flyway visit lion's tail repeatedly. Extremely drought-tolerant once established, requiring little supplemental water in zone 9 after the first season. Fast-growing from seed, potentially blooming in the first year. The traditional South African medicinal plant known as 'wilde dagga' (wild hemp) — no relation to cannabis.

planting.note: Plant bulbs 2 inches deep in well-drained, moderately fertile, slightly acidic to neutral, sandy soil in full sun to part shade; Grand Duchess sorrel follows the Cape winter-rainfall cycle — growing and blooming in winter and spring, going completely summer dormant. Keep bone dry in summer. Several color forms are in cultivation — 'Grand Duchess White', 'Grand Duchess Lavender', 'Grand Duchess Pink' — all sold as selections of this species. The flowers are notably large for the genus, opening to 1.5–2 inches across.

directions: Plant bulbs in well-drained, moderately fertile, slightly acidic to neutral, sandy soil in full sun to part shade; Grand Duchess sorrel is among the most prolific and showy of all the winter-growing Cape oxalis — producing from winter through spring an extended succession of large (1.5–2 inch), flat-faced, funnel-shaped flowers in clear, vivid purple to rose-purple to lavender or white (depending on the selected form), each flower with a contrasting yellow center, rising above compact, clover-like, dark-green foliage in a continuous, profuse bloom display that can last 3–4 months of winter and early spring. The flowers are substantially larger than most Cape oxalis species, giving the plant a presence disproportionate to its small bulb size. It has been selected into named color forms ('Grand Duchess White', 'Grand Duchess Lavender', 'Grand Duchess Pink') sold in the bulb trade and widely grown in California and Mediterranean-climate gardens. In zone 9 Gulf Coast gardens, it provides a bright, low-maintenance, winter-through-spring color display that requires almost no attention — plant the bulbs in fall, enjoy the flowers from winter through April, water withheld in summer. One of the most rewarding and easy-to-grow winter bulbs for warm-climate gardens.

planting.note: Plant container-grown plants in very well-drained, lean to moderately fertile, slightly acidic to neutral, sandy to rocky soil in full sun; breath of heaven requires excellent drainage — it declines quickly in wet, heavy, or poorly-drained soils. Do not over-fertilize; rich soils reduce flowering and shorten plant life. Prune lightly after flowering to maintain compact shape. The aromatic foliage releases a sweet citrus-like scent when touched. Not tolerant of salt spray.

directions: Plant in very well-drained, lean, slightly acidic to neutral, sandy or rocky soil in full sun; breath of heaven is a South African Cape fynbos shrub prized for two complementary qualities: the fine, soft, needle-like, heather-like aromatic foliage that releases a sweet citrus-like scent when brushed or touched — earning the common name 'breath of heaven' — and the winter to early spring flowering, when the entire shrub becomes covered in hundreds of tiny, five-petaled, pink to white-pink flowers so numerous and so densely covering the fine, feathery foliage that the plant appears dusted with confetti, giving the alternative common name 'confetti bush'. The flower display in late winter and early spring, when the fine-textured shrub is smothered in small, clear-pink flowers against the bright-green, aromatic, needle-like foliage, is one of the most cheerful winter garden displays available in zone 9 landscapes. The aromatic foliage is attractive year-round and the light, fine texture provides a delicate contrast to bolder neighboring plants. It demands excellent drainage — the primary requirement for success — and lean soils. A valued landscape shrub in California and in Gulf Coast Mediterranean-style gardens.

planting.note: Plant container-grown plants or rooted cuttings in very well-drained, lean, slightly acidic to neutral, sandy or rocky soil in full sun; buchu requires excellent drainage and lean conditions — it is native to the sandy, peaty fynbos soils of the Western Cape mountains. Avoid heavy clay or overly rich soils. The strongly aromatic leaves (dried and fresh) have been used in South African traditional medicine for centuries and are the source of a commercial herbal supplement with urinary and anti-inflammatory properties.

directions: Plant in very well-drained, lean, slightly acidic to neutral, sandy or rocky soil in full sun; buchu is one of the most important medicinal plants of the South African Cape fynbos and one of the few Cape plants to have achieved significant international commercial significance as a herbal supplement. The small, oval, dark-green leaves are powerfully aromatic — the fresh or dried leaf releasing a strong, distinctive, somewhat blackcurrant-minty-spicy scent from oil glands visible as transparent dots in the leaf surface. In traditional San and Khoikhoi medicine, and later in European colonial medicine, buchu was used primarily for urinary tract and kidney complaints, and dried buchu leaf extract is still sold today as a supplement for urinary health and anti-inflammatory purposes. As a garden plant it is a neat, bushy, heather-textured small shrub of the fynbos type — small, oval, aromatic leaves on fine stems — that produces in spring small, white, five-petaled flowers in moderate quantity. The aromatic foliage is the primary ornamental and practical value. Excellent for a dedicated medicinal herb garden or Cape fynbos planting in zones 8–11 where its drainage and lean-soil requirements are met.

subcategory: spectacular winter-flowering South African small tree

planting.note: Plant container-grown plants in well-drained, moderately fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; Natal bottlebrush tree flowers spectacularly in late winter and early spring (August–October in South Africa) on bare branches before the leaves appear. In the Northern Hemisphere it flowers January–April. Deciduous in cooler zones; semi-evergreen in frost-free conditions. Outstanding for hummingbirds and sunbirds. Grows slowly to 10–15 feet in favorable conditions. Named for Sir George Grey, Governor of the Cape Colony.

directions: Plant in well-drained, moderately fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; Natal bottlebrush tree is a spectacular South African small tree or large shrub that produces in late winter, on bare leafless branches (flowering before the leaves emerge — a behavior called 'hysteranthous' that creates an unusually dramatic floral display against the bare winter framework of the tree), dense, cylindrical clusters of flowers in vivid scarlet to crimson-red, with prominent, projecting red stamens creating the brushy, bottle-brush-like texture. The individual flowers are composed primarily of conspicuous red stamens projecting from small scarlet sepals, creating the bottlebrush silhouette of the common name, and the color is an intense, clear, fire-engine red. A mature tree in full bloom — red bottlebrush clusters covering every leafless branch against the winter sky — is one of the most dramatically colorful winter spectacles in the zone 9 garden, and an irresistible feeding station for winter hummingbirds. The large, rounded, somewhat fig-like leaves that follow the flowers are also attractive. Related to Melianthus but without the strong odor. A choice specimen tree for warm gardens seeking a spectacular winter-blooming feature tree.
